title: Piles: comfort and what helps
excerpt: Arsha, the Ayurvedic term for haemorrhoids, has troubled people for centuries, and the classical texts offer a grounded, practical way to understand and ease it through food, daily habits, and gentle care.
at_a_glance: Arsha (haemorrhoids) is linked to chronic constipation and straining, a connection Sushruta described over two thousand years ago.
Warm water, moist lightly spiced food, and a warm sitz soak are the most practical classical recommendations for everyday comfort.
Triphala churna at bedtime and a short walk after meals are the two simplest habits the texts return to for supporting bowel regularity.

body.heading: what Ayurveda calls arsha
paragraphs: If you have ever spent a few uncomfortable minutes in the bathroom and walked out feeling sore, bloated, or uneasy, you already know the territory this article is about. Piles, known in Ayurveda as Arsha (literally, something that torments like an enemy), are swollen tissue masses at or near the opening of the rectum. They can bleed, itch, or simply cause a dull heaviness that follows you through the day.

Sushruta, the ancient surgeon whose Sushruta Samhita is one of Ayurveda's foundational texts, described Arsha in considerable detail and classified it as one of the eight Mahagadas, the great diseases that are difficult to manage (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana). He observed that swollen folds of rectal tissue block the passage of wind, stool, and urine, and that over time this leads to weakness and distress throughout the body. The Bhavaprakasha, a later but equally respected text, echoes this view and connects chronic Arsha to poor digestive fire (Agni) and accumulated waste (Mala) that is not passed easily (Bhavaprakasha, Chikitsa Prakarana).

body.heading: the constipation connection
paragraphs: In classical Ayurvedic understanding, Arsha and Vibandha (constipation) are deeply linked. Sushruta notes that habitual straining at stool, sitting for long periods on hard or low seats, and eating food that is too dry, too heavy, or too spicy all aggravate the condition (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana). The Chakradatta, a clinical text focused on treatment, adds that irregular meal timings, suppression of natural urges, and a sedentary routine weaken the downward-moving energy (Apana Vata) that governs evacuation, making the stool hard and passage difficult (Chakradatta, Arshoghna Chikitsa).

From a modern perspective, this maps closely to what gastroenterologists observe: straining during constipation increases pressure in the rectal veins, and over time the tissue there becomes engorged and prolapsed. The Ayurvedic insight is not that different from the fibre-and-fluid advice you hear from a family doctor, but it places it inside a broader picture of digestive rhythm and lifestyle.

body.heading: everyday habits that help
paragraphs: The practical recommendations in the classical texts are surprisingly accessible. The Bhavaprakasha recommends foods that are light, moist, and easy to digest: cooked vegetables, thin lentil soups (moong dal in particular), buttermilk, and warm water throughout the day (Bhavaprakasha, Chikitsa Prakarana). Dry, fried, very spicy, and stale foods are specifically named as things to reduce. This is not about a strict diet but about tipping your daily eating in a gentler direction.

Warm water is mentioned repeatedly across the texts as a simple but important support. Starting the morning with a glass or two of warm water, and sipping warm water rather than cold drinks through the day, helps soften stool and support Apana Vata. The Chakradatta also recommends a sitz-style warm water soak for external discomfort: sitting in a basin of warm water for ten to fifteen minutes can ease soreness and reduce swelling around the anal region (Chakradatta, Arshoghna Chikitsa). No special equipment is needed, just a wide basin you can sit comfortably in.

Triphala, a combination of three fruits (Amalaki, Bibhitaki, and Haritaki), is the most commonly mentioned formulation across Bhavaprakasha and Chakradatta for supporting bowel regularity in Arsha. A teaspoon of Triphala churna mixed in warm water at bedtime is a gentle, traditional way to ease constipation without strong laxatives. It is widely available and generally well tolerated, though it is worth checking with a vaidya or doctor for the right dose if you are managing other conditions. Walking for twenty to thirty minutes after meals, avoiding sitting on the floor or on very hard surfaces for long stretches, and not suppressing the urge to go when it arises are the small daily habits the texts come back to again and again.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: This article is general information and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. If you notice bright red bleeding from the rectum, especially if it is more than a few drops, you should see a doctor promptly. The same applies if the bleeding is dark or mixed into the stool, if you have pain that is severe or getting worse, if you feel a lump that will not go back in, if you have unexplained weight loss or a change in bowel habits that has lasted more than a few weeks, or if you are over forty and experiencing rectal bleeding for the first time. A qualified vaidya or Ayurvedic physician can help guide classical treatment, but a medical evaluation is important to rule out other causes before attributing symptoms to Arsha. Most piles respond well to conservative care, but some cases need clinical intervention, and getting a proper assessment is always the right first step.

try_this_today.title: Warm water first thing
try_this_today.description: Drink one to two glasses of warm water on an empty stomach each morning. This gently stimulates the bowel and softens stool, making the first trip to the bathroom easier.

try_this_today.title: Warm sitz soak
try_this_today.description: Fill a wide basin with comfortably warm (not hot) water and sit in it for ten to fifteen minutes after a bowel movement or when soreness is worst. Pat dry gently with a soft cloth.

try_this_today.title: Triphala at bedtime
try_this_today.description: Mix half to one teaspoon of Triphala churna in a cup of warm water and drink it about thirty minutes before bed. It supports regularity without harsh laxative effects. Check with your doctor or vaidya first if you are on other medications.

try_this_today.title: Shift toward moist, easy foods
try_this_today.description: For a week, try replacing one dry or fried snack each day with cooked moong dal, a bowl of khichdi, or a glass of diluted buttermilk with a pinch of roasted jeera. Notice whether your digestion feels lighter.

try_this_today.title: Walk after meals
try_this_today.description: A gentle fifteen to twenty minute walk after lunch or dinner helps move food through and supports Apana Vata. You do not need to walk fast: a calm, unhurried pace is enough.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: Roasted cumin added to buttermilk is a traditional digestive support mentioned in classical kitchens and texts.

words_to_know.definition: The Ayurvedic term for haemorrhoids, literally meaning something that torments like an enemy.
The downward-moving sub-type of Vata energy that governs elimination, evacuation, and the lower abdomen.
Constipation in Ayurveda, considered a primary cause and aggravating factor of Arsha.
Digestive fire, the transformative capacity of the gut that determines how well food is processed and waste is cleared.
A classical Ayurvedic formulation of three fruits (Amalaki, Bibhitaki, Haritaki) used to support bowel regularity.
Bodily waste products, including stool, that need to be eliminated regularly to maintain health.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana, Arsha Nidana · Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Arsha Chikitsa · Bhavaprakasha, Chikitsa Prakarana, Arsha Adhyaya · Chakradatta, Arshoghna Chikitsa
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
